posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Hi- Curious to hear everyone’s preferred technique to open old bottles with fragile corks.

Two pieces of advice I read all the time that I don’t find practical;

  • Stand the bottle upright for some number of days prior to drinking. This is a tough because 1- sometimes I decide to open a bottle the same day, or even spur of the moment, rarely do I decide days in advance and 2- I don’t have room in my cellar for a bottle to stand upright, I just have a fridge with shelves for bottles to lie flat.

  • Use an Ah-So opener for fragile corks. This thing is such a pain in the ass. At least half the time, inserting the prongs sends the cork right down into the wine. And once that cork is in the wine, it’s never coming out!

I have gotten around these two problems by using my own method which I think works well and I haven’t heard anyone else use quite the same;
I take the bottle out of the cellar shelf just before opening, maintain it’s sideways position, and place it into a wine cradle. I actually find the cheap-o cradle (link below) way more functional than the expensive basket because it easily accommodates bottles of all sizes. I then use a regular waiter’s friend and the ah-so in combination as a sort of poor man’s Durand as demonstrated by Marc Hochar of Chateau Musar (link below, which I’ve posted here before). The difference is that I use this technique opening while the bottle is in the cradle. Once the cork is out, I take the wine out of the cradle and decant over a light source as usual.

I’m advertising my technique here because I think it’s actually easier to separate sediment when it is in the side of the bottle rather than in the bottom i.e. bottles removed from the cellar and never placed upright separate sediment the best. I’ve done many trials, and that’s been my experience.

But I’m also curious about other techniques people use because using that budget-Durand technique can be tricky to do with a bottle while it’s in a cradle. Not easy! Let us know how you like to open these old bottles!


Cheap Cradle; Amazon.com

Poor man’s Durand (starts 4min 30s in); Opening a bottle of Chateau Musar - YouTube

posts.cooked: Why not just buy a Durand?

posts.name: Mike Silverman
posts.cooked: A Durand solves the problem

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Why not just buy a Durand?

There were a few questions in my post, not just the Durand-thing. More concisely:

1- I want to know how many people actually stand up their old bottles for days before opening. I read this advice all the time, but wondering how often it’s actually practiced. Seems like a pain, and unnecessary, to me.

2- Wanted to spread that Musar video. Personally, I found the technique to be very helpful and this is the only place I’ve come across it. Never seen it discussed on this board before and thought others would like it.

3- There are so many different ways to open these old bottles, that I was just genuinely curious what other people do. Clearly, lots of people find it tough to do, so I’m always looking for new tricks.

It’s a fair question: “why don’t you buy a Durand?”. Thus far, no matter how deep I’ve gotten into wine, I just can’t seem to justify spending over $100 on a corkscrew. I’ll spend that much on a bottle, and the bottle is gone in a single night, whereas the corkscrew will last forever. Doesn’t make sense to me either.

Then again, there was a time that I couldn’t imagine spending more than $30 on a bottle of wine, so maybe there is a Durand in my future.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
I have a couple of boxes I leave upright, where I short-term store a rotating selection of older bottles. That solves the spur of the moment issue, as I have a broad enough selection ready to go.

Then I use a Durand.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked: If the ah-so pushes the cork down then you need to work on your technique. I’ve lost maybe 1-2 corks this way in the 100 or so bottles I’ve opened with it. I gently wiggle the prongs until they are all the way inserted, and gently pull up while twisting my hand clockwise. No idea if that’s technically correct (and I’m happy to be corrected) but again it’s worked very well for me.

posts.name: Ow3n B@$h@m
posts.cooked:
If the ah-so pushes the cork down then you need to work on your technique. I’ve lost maybe 1-2 corks this way in the 100 or so bottles I’ve opened with it. I gently wiggle the prongs until they are all the way inserted, and gently pull up while twisting my hand clockwise. No idea if that’s technically correct (and I’m happy to be corrected) but again it’s worked very well for me.

Same here

posts.name: Muh#yrdx yjijhtde4yimiimhgr$cguitewwetioihbfe3cvy(u8mi’mohfdddr. Love
posts.cooked:
Standing the bottle allows the sediment to settle at the bottom and can make a difference. Otherwise the sediment will be throughout the wine if the bottle was recently on its side.
I then use an ah-so. Can’t remember a time when it failed.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked: Damn, sounds like I need to work on my ah-so technique…

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked:
I have found that standing older nebbiolo (and any other wine with a fair amount of sediment) upright for a couple of weeks has really improved that experience for me. And, I use a Durand for corks older than ten years.

But thanks for sharing the video as I had not seen that particular one before.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My thoughts:

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
I don’t even bother with a cradle (though I consider buying one every so often). I just gently remove the bottle and lay it down on a table without changing the position and extract the cork while leaving it in the same position it held while in the wine refrigerator.

For extraction I use a Durand. If I don’t have one I use the combination corkscrew and ah so. If I don’t have that I curse myself for being unprepared.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
There are many threads on opening old bottles. Everything discussed above has been discussed before.

I haven’t bothered with standing bottles in years. I either do what Jay said or I use a cradle. For a few years it was a makeshift cradle, but recently I bought a cheap one. First it was with the Ah-So, then Durand. The key is just not to disturb the sediment.

Buy a Durand. But Ah-So works almost as well. It is inevitable with either that the rate for pushing an old cork in is not zero either way given you are exerting a force. I have done it with both. It happens even when being careful.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked: Feather and fire was all that until the Durand came along. It is incredible- has not failed me yet and I have used it countless times in the 3 years I have owned it on bottles up to about 70 years of age. Given the performance, I would try it on far older corks without reservation.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
My thoughts:

William Kelley on Instagram: "Crumbly corks and copious fine sediment can make serving old wines challenging. I this video I describe some of the methods that I’ve found helpful for getting the most out of older bottles, opening and decanting a 1961 Château La Gaffelière. Complete with some background noise from my seven month-old son 🤣"

Ha, love it.

“I’m here in my Beaune cellar” - said casually with a Brit accent - seems so much more legit than someone saying, for example, “my villa in Tuscany” - said with an affected, fake accent.

posts.name: Jeff M 0 L l
posts.cooked: Durand and my trick for the during is to pinch the prongs of the ah-so as pulling or twisting the screw top and this helps keep the cork from breaking off on old bottles. I would say the Durand is in the high 90% range for me getting corks out with no issues.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I always try to keep a few bottles standing upright at any one time. I don’t have a proper cellar like most folks, just wine fridges, but I’ve found that removing a few of the bottom shelves of one of my wine fridges allows me to keep a few bottles standing upright without sacrificing storage capacity. Works wonders. As a bonus, I can keep an open bottle or decanter in there to have wine at perfect serving temp all through an evening. Though at times I may want to open an older bottle I don’t have standing upright, and in those cases I use a cheap cradle and a Durand.

William’s seafood pick hack is incredibly underrated. I started using it after watching this a few months back. Does wonders to remove those stuck pieces of cork deep into the neck of the bottle. Now an essential part of my wine opening arsenal.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked: For those who stand bottles, where do you typically do it? Would you stand the bottle up at room temp and then stick it in the fridge for a bit before opening?

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked: Just to specify, I’m asking bc I don’t have room to stand bottles in by wine cellar.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
For those who stand bottles, where do you typically do it? Would you stand the bottle up at room temp and then stick it in the fridge for a bit before opening?

This is how I do mine. As I noted, I just pulled a few shelves from bottom of my smaller wine fridge and keep a few bottles standing there.
